title: Will the unemployment rate (U-3) be above 4.1% in June?

current.lean_note: 55pt gap exceeds the hard ceiling (20pt) — beyond this divergence from a liquid market, even high confidence is treated as model error, not edge
current.rationale_summary: The evidence suggests mixed signals about the U.S. labor market in May 2026. While some reports indicate strong job gains and stable unemployment rates, others highlight underlying labor market slack and regional variations. The fact that Hawaii's unemployment rate was 2.5% in May might suggest a relatively strong labor market in some regions, but it is not representative of the national trend. Th
key_drivers: Mixed signals from labor market reports (both positive and negative)
Regional variations in unemployment and job growth
The potential for headline jobs numbers to mask underlying labor market slack
The importance of alternative measures like U-6 in understanding labor underutilization
reference_classes: Historical U.S. labor market trends with periods of strong job growth and labor market slack
Regional unemployment rates and their impact on national statistics

history.rationale_summary: U-3 has held at 4.3% for April and May 2026; resolving NO requires a rare 0.2pp single-month drop against Iran-war and tariff headwinds and rising jobless claims.

history.lean_note: I lean YES (my prob 92%) but it isn't +EV at the ask — no value bet (betting the other side would contradict my forecast).
history.rationale_summary: May 2026 U-3 = 4.3%, in a 4.3-4.5% range since Jul 2025; no sign of a drop below 4.1% in June. Very likely above 4.1%.

history.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 93%, modal YES) — staying out.
history.rationale_summary: U-3 has printed 4.3-4.5% every month since mid-2025 (May 4.3%, June consensus 4.3-4.4%); a sub-4.1% print needs a multi-tenth downside surprise with no catalyst.
